### Post by Hopeless on 2007-09-05
Hi all,

Where do i put these?

Something like the following should be in your main firewall script:

---cut---

[COLOR="Red"]iptables -F TREASON
iptables -X TREASON
iptables -N TREASON[/COLOR]

... (your rest of the rules)

[COLOR="Red"]iptables -j TREASON[/COLOR] # insert before state established and other lines

---cut---

my firewall..
# Generated by iptables-save v1.3.6 on Wed Aug 29 17:37:14 2007
*filter
:INPUT ACCEPT [413:23830]
:FORWARD ACCEPT [0:0]
:OUTPUT ACCEPT [433:136061]
-A INPUT -m state --state RELATED,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT
-A INPUT -i eth0 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT
-A INPUT -i eth0 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 80 -j ACCEPT
-A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT
-A INPUT -j DROP
COMMIT
# Completed on Wed Aug 29 17:37:14 2007

many thanks...

### Post by banewman on 2007-09-05
OK. Which firewall are you using? If you don't have firestarter or gaurdian as firewalls then the best option is to set up rules in iptables.
Maybe that is why your having trouble finding your firewall config file. Come back and let us know which - if any - firewall you have and we will go from there. :)

### Post by banewman on 2007-09-05
From reading "man iptables" the lines you wanted to put in were not suitable for iptables. That's why I asked about the firewall you were using. Apologies. To block an attack the entry you need is - 
```
 iptables -A INPUT -s (the IP address from dmesg) -j DROP
```
This will put the offending address first in the chains and block any input they can have to your comp. If you do this and then can't connect to the net the IP address was from your ISP, but from the sites I've read that is a 50/50 chance. Some say the treason line is an error, others a dos attack. Hope this helps even though it doesn't tell you where to put those lies. :lolflag:

---

### Post by Hopeless on 2007-09-06
yeah i know so far i got a file named treason:
```
#!/bin/bash

for ATTACKER_IP in $(dmesg | grep 'Treason uncloaked!' | cut -d' ' -f5 | cut -d':' -f1 | sort --unique)
do
iptables -A INPUT -s $ATTACKER_IP -j DROP
done
```

make the script executable:
Code:

```
chmod +x treason
```

run the script:
Code:

```
./treason
```

but would like the better version in the link...

### Post by banewman on 2007-09-07
You've missed a point here : dmesg will show an internet address on the same line as treason. That internet address is the one you use when setting a rule in iptables. At terminal type - man iptables - and you will see the syntax for setting a rule for your firewall. The word treason can't be in a rule. Can you paste the lines from dmesg that have treason in them and I will give you the terminal entry to set up iptables to block the address. :)

---
